mappings.rationaleAI: Catherine II's reign directly covers A.3.d (further westernization and expansion, partitions of Poland, successful wars against the Ottoman Empire) and A.3.e (education and social change under the Enlightenment's impact in Russia).

Suleiman I's reign (1520-1566) is explicitly named in B.1.a.i as the Ottoman Empire's conflicts with the Habsburgs in Hungary and annexation of Iraq; his role as the Ottoman Empire's peak ruler is the core content of B.1.a.

The Ottoman Empire article is the primary source for all of B.1, covering the empire's dominance under Bayezid II through Suleiman (B.1.a), classical Ottoman society and administration (B.1.a.ii), decline and corruption 1566-1807 (B.1.b), and imperial disintegration in the 18th-early 19th centuries (B.1.c).

Covers the entire 1500-1800 period for Eastern Europe, the Ottoman Empire, Safavid Iran, and other regions addressed in Section 962, including nation-state formation, globalization onset, and secularization across A and B.

Russia's history directly covers the emergence of the Russian Empire — Muscovite expansion under Ivan IV (A.3.a), the Time of Troubles and Romanov election (A.3.a-b), Peter the Great's westernization (A.3.c), and Catherine II's further expansion (A.3.d).

Iran's history under the Safavids — Shi'ite dynasty, conflict with Turks, Abbas I (1587-1629), and decline (B.2.a.i) — and the Nadir Shah period, expulsion of Afghans, and establishment of Zand and Qajar dynasties (B.2.a.ii) directly covers the full B.2.a outline path.

Poland's history covers the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth's Golden Age under Jagiellonian rule (A.1.a), the royal republic period (A.1.b), the Swedish and Cossack wars (A.1.c), the Saxon era, Russian protectorate, and three partitions 1772-1795 (A.1.d) — the full A.1 path.

Nadir Shah's invasion of India (1738-39) is explicitly named in B.2.a.ii; the Mughal Empire article provides context for the target and consequences of this invasion, which disrupted both Mughal and Iranian political trajectories.

The outline explicitly names effects of the Reformation as a domestic development in Polish-Lithuania (A.1.a.ii) and the spread of Protestantism in Hungary (A.2.b); the Reformation article covers the Counter-Reformation and confessional geography that shaped both of these paths.

The Enlightenment's impact on Russia is explicitly named in A.3.e (education and social change, impact of the Enlightenment), and Catherine II's Enlightenment reforms are central to A.3.d, making this article substantively relevant to the Russian dimension of Section 962.

The Byzantine Empire's fall to the Ottomans — Constantinople conquered 1453, just before this section's c.1480 start — provides direct context for Ottoman consolidation and the empire's role as the dominant power in Southeast Europe and Southwest Asia (B.1.a).

The Habsburg dimension of the Holy Roman Empire directly overlaps with Hungary's partition period (A.2.b) — Habsburg sectors controlling part of Hungary after Mohacs (1526) — and the Austro-Turkish wars that shaped the Hungarian section of this outline.

The outline explicitly names effects of the Renaissance as a domestic development in Poland-Lithuania's Golden Age (A.1.a.ii); the Renaissance article covers its spread to Poland and other Central European states during this period.

Classical Ottoman society and administration (B.1.a.ii) drew on Islamic Golden Age intellectual and legal traditions; the article covers the Islamic world's cultural production that informed Ottoman civil and religious law structures in the 15th-16th centuries.

The Mongol conquest and Golden Horde's role in shaping Russian state formation is background to A.3.a (Muscovite expansion and control of Russia), as the overthrow of Mongol-Tatar authority was the foundational context for Ivan IV's centralisation.
